syntaxReference: Declare primitives: int n = 0; double d = 3.14; boolean ok = true;
Declare references: Point p; p = new Point(0,0);
Array creation: int[] a = new int[5]; int[] b = {1,2,3};
Copy arrays: int[] c = Arrays.copyOf(a, a.length); int[] d = a.clone();
String equality: s1.equals(s2); // not s1 == s2
Method parameters: void f(int x, Point p) { /* pass-by-value */ }
Deep copy sketch: Point q = new Point(p.x, p.y); // copy fields
commonPatterns: Defensive copy of mutable input: void setData(int[] data){ this.data = Arrays.copyOf(data, data.length); }
Return new instead of mutating argument: Point moved(Point p){ return new Point(p.x + 1, p.y); }
Builder-style or copy constructor for deep copy: Matrix(Matrix m){ /* copy rows with Arrays.copyOf */ }
quickTips: Draw small stack/heap diagrams to predict side effects.
Prefer immutable data (e.g., Strings) to reduce aliasing bugs.
When in doubt, test with prints to see whether two variables reference the same object (System.out.println(a == b)).
